title: Lap-level probability-mass normalization using empirical pit-count priors
summary: Pit calls are not independent across rows; on a given race lap, usually zero or a small cluster of cars pit.
rationale: Pit calls are not independent across rows; on a given race lap, usually zero or a small cluster of cars pit. F1 strategy articles emphasize pit windows, undercut range, and clustered reactions once a window opens. Modeling each row independently misses that competition structure.
implementation_hint: From training folds only, estimate expected pit-count distributions by coarse contexts such as `(race_progress_bin, compound, stint, wet/dry, track regime)`. After base predictions, normalize probabilities within each `(Race, Year, LapNumber)` so total mass matches the context prior, or blend a lap-level classifier predicting `number_of_pitters_next_lap` and rescale row probabilities accordingly.
expected_effect: Should reduce overprediction on quiet laps and sharpen predictions on tactical cluster laps, improving leaderboard robustness more than local CV alone.
risk: Wrong priors can flatten true signals. Must be OOF-built and soft, not hard constrained.
